purpose: Implementation framework for the MCP 'backpacks' carried by the 16 OPSEC Gauntlet sector judges. Each judge is a board-level, sector-aligned evaluator; the backpack is the portable live-tool capability that travels with the character (the traveling-character rule).
design_rules: Traveling characters carry their own backpack: the tools below belong to the judge, not to a single site framework.
Read-only. No write, no transaction, no actuation against any real system.
Public and open sources only: government registries, regulator data, standards bodies. No classified, proprietary, or personal data.
Backpacks are advisory: they return regulatory, safety, and resilience CONTEXT, never operational attack detail.
Until backpack_shipped flips true, the judge runs on static sector knowledge; the price holds at 89.99 either way.
global_guardrails: Provide only regulatory, safety, and resilience context.
Never provide synthesis, exploit, intrusion, sabotage, or weaponization detail.
Never identify specific exploitable weaknesses, soft targets, or protected locations.
Never handle classified, proprietary, or personal data.
Any request that resembles causing harm is refused and escalated, not answered.

escalation_policy.on_trigger: Stop the sector analysis, refuse the operational detail, and return an escalation notice to the OPSEC Gauntlet intake (Iris S. King / Office of Frontline Communications) for human review.
shared_triggers: harm to people or critical infrastructure

tool_type_legend.reference_api: Live but used as reference context, not monitoring.
tool_type_legend.live_feed: Continuously updated public feed (e.g., outbreak/alert notices).

judges.name: Dr. Helena Ward

tools.source: EPA RMP*Info via Envirofacts REST API
tools.notes: Risk Management Plan facilities, hazards, accident history.

tools.source: EPA Toxics Release Inventory (TRI) Envirofacts API
tools.notes: Facility-level chemical release and handling data.
Classify a substance's hazard class; no handling or synthesis detail.

tools.source: DEA Diversion Control List I / List II (public lists)
tools.notes: Identifies whether a material is a regulated precursor. Context only.

tools.source: PHMSA 49 CFR HMR + public incident data
tools.notes: Transport/packaging requirements and historical incident patterns.

guardrails: Never provides quantities, ratios, or reaction steps
Only describes regulatory and safety context
Escalates any question that resembles weaponization

tools.notes: Confirms registration, CAGE code, exclusions.
Federal award history for a vendor or competitor.

tools.source: trade.gov Consolidated Screening List API + BIS Entity List
tools.notes: Denied/blocked parties and foreign-influence flags.
Whether an item/transaction is export-controlled. Never advises bypass.
Provenance risk patterns; GIDEP itself is access-controlled and out of scope.

tools.notes: Licensed facilities and structures near an operation.
Prefix, AS path, and routing-anomaly signals. Public route data only.
Upstream diversity and single-point-of-failure mapping. No private fiber routes.
Historical outage patterns by region/cause.
Band occupancy context. Never advises jamming or interference.

Escalates any request to degrade communications

Public filings for diligence on a vendor or partner.

tools.source: FinCEN advisories + FTC/CFPB public complaint data
tools.notes: Known fraud typologies. Never AML-evasion or laundering patterns.

tools.source: Public breach + enforcement records (cross-ref CISA KEV, FTC actions)
tools.notes: Vendor exposure context. Never touches personal financial data.

guardrails: Escalates any request to bypass sanctions

tools.notes: Recalls and enforcement on a device class.
Public-health alerts relevant to continuity planning.
Active shortages affecting a care operation.
Healthcare-sector continuity and threat context.
Cold-chain integrity standards. No diagnosis, treatment, or patient data.

tools.notes: Active and historical incident context by area.
Live alerts feeding readiness assessment.
Real-time natural-hazard signals.

Facility compliance and enforcement history.

specialists.name: Dr. Sahini Rao
specialists.office: Office of Dual Use Systems Analysis (second stop)
specialists.mission: Determine whether an idea touches controlled CBRN or national-security dual-use territory, classify it against the authoritative control regimes, and surface current threat-awareness context, so the concept can be routed and mitigated. Classification and awareness only.

tools.source: OPCW — Chemical Weapons Convention Annex on Chemicals
tools.notes: Whether a substance is a CWC-scheduled chemical.
Precursor and dual-use manufacturing-equipment controls.

tools.source: CISA Chemicals of Interest (CFATS Appendix A) + ATSDR agent data
tools.notes: Risk reference; note CFATS statutory authority lapsed 2023, list still indicative.

tools.source: HHS/USDA Federal Select Agent Program list
tools.notes: Whether an agent/toxin is federally regulated as a select agent.
Human/animal/plant pathogen and dual-use bio-equipment controls.

tools.source: IGSC Harmonized Screening Protocol + HHS synthetic-nucleic-acid screening guidance
tools.notes: Sequence-of-concern screening framework for synthesis providers.

tools.source: WHO Disease Outbreak News + CDC Health Alert Network (HAN)
tools.notes: Up-to-date biological-event awareness. Defensive context only.
Treaty-level prohibition and obligation context.

tools.source: Nuclear Suppliers Group Trigger List + Dual-Use List
tools.notes: Whether an item is NSG-controlled.

tools.source: IAEA Code of Conduct (Category 1-2) + NRC source security
tools.notes: Risk-significant radioactive source categorization.

tools.source: IAEA Incident and Trafficking Database (ITDB) public summaries
tools.notes: Current radiological/nuclear material trafficking context.

tools.source: NRC license & ADAMS lookup + IAEA INFCIRC safeguards
tools.notes: Public licensing records and international safeguards context.

tools.source: Missile Technology Control Regime (MTCR) Annex Cat I/II
tools.notes: Delivery-system technology controls.

tools.source: BIS Commerce Control List / EAR (15 CFR 774) + Federal Register rule updates
tools.notes: ECCN classification and current rulemaking, incl. Section 1758 emerging/foundational tech.
Military/civilian crossover screening.
Multilateral dual-use control categories.

tools.source: BIS Entity List + trade.gov Consolidated Screening List
tools.notes: Denied/blocked parties and end-user concerns.

tools.source: UN Security Council 1540 Committee + UN sanctions
tools.notes: State obligations to prevent WMD proliferation to non-state actors.

tools.source: ODNI Annual Threat Assessment + public NCTC/DNI products
tools.notes: Current strategic CBRN and nat-sec threat framing.

tools.source: CRS + GAO CBRN / nonproliferation reports + CISA advisories
tools.notes: Authoritative analysis and advisory context.
Misuse and adversarial-reinterpretation patterns, incl. AI-enabled.

tools.source: NIST AI Risk Management Framework + GenAI profile
tools.notes: Frames AI-CBRN uplift risk for dual-use evaluation.
Disclosure, cybersecurity, and research-security program obligations.

tools.source: NIH (foreign component / FCOI) + NSF research-security policies
tools.notes: Funder-specific disclosure and foreign-interference requirements.

tools.source: US Policy for Oversight of DURC + P3CO (2024 unified policy)
tools.notes: DURC and enhanced-PPP review obligations for life-sciences research.

tools.source: NARA CUI program (32 CFR 2002) + NIST SP 800-171
tools.notes: Handling controls for CUI in research environments.
When research crosses into deemed-export or controlled territory.
Foreign-influence review touchpoints for technology and institutions.

permissions: Classify items against CBRN and dual-use control lists (CWC/OPCW, Australia Group, NSG, MTCR, Wassenaar, EAR/CCL, ITAR/USML, Select Agents)
Surface current threat-awareness context (WHO DON, CDC HAN, IAEA ITDB, ODNI threat assessment)
Restricted-party and WMD-nonproliferation screening (Entity List, CSL, UNSCR 1540)
Repurposing and adversarial-reinterpretation framing (MITRE ATT&CK/ATLAS, NIST AI RMF)
Map research-security obligations (NSPM-33, DURC/P3CO, deemed exports, CUI/800-171, foreign-influence review)
guardrails: Provides only control-status, regulatory classification, threat-awareness context, and mitigation framing.
Never provides synthesis, production, acquisition, enhancement, or weaponization detail for any chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear material.
Never identifies acquisition sources, facility vulnerabilities, or specific exploitable pathways.
Treats any request that seeks CBRN or WMD uplift as an immediate refusal and escalation.
Read-only official and public sources only; no classified, proprietary, or personal data.

specialists.name: Dr. Ali Malik
specialists.mission: Map what an adversary can learn about a participant or organization from open sources alone, then show what to close. Exposure mapping only.

tools.notes: Who registered a domain and when.
Subdomains and certs that reveal infrastructure.
What a site exposed in the past, including deleted pages.
Open ports, services, and devices facing the internet.
Whether a domain or account appears in known breaches.
Company ownership, officers, and filings.
Federal registration and award history.
Sanctioned and politically-exposed entity screening.
Place, address, and asset geolocation.
Public imagery for site context.
Public aircraft movement data.
Worldwide event and media signal monitoring.
Public federal and state court records.
Public patent and inventor footprint.

permissions: Corporate, federal, sanctions, and court-record lookup
guardrails: Maps the participant's own exposure or an organization's footprint; never builds a targeting or surveillance package on a private individual.
Never deanonymizes, locates, or profiles a person for harm.
Refuses and escalates any request that resembles stalking, doxxing, or targeting.
Read-only public sources only; no intrusion and no credential use.
escalation_triggers: targeting or surveillance of an individual

workflow.sequence_position: Final desk at the end of the corridor, after Iris greets, Ivy, Rao, the specialist offices, and the sector judges.
workflow.opt_in: Surfaced by Iris as a natural aside on the Corridor landing page (collapsed identifier form), not at his desk.
workflow.async_prefetch: If accepted, the sweep starts at opt-in and runs in the background while the participant walks the other offices, so results are ready by the time they reach his desk. He needs the lead time; do not run it on-demand at the desk.
workflow.at_desk: His office opens straight to the flanking layout (no gate). Brief panel is pre-populated if the visitor opted in via Iris, otherwise it prompts for the subject.

data_handling.assurance_to_user: Nothing found stays on the net or with ETL. The sweep only reads what is already public, never republishes or indexes it, and ETL retains none of it after your session.
data_handling.no_republication: Reads public sources only; never posts, indexes, mirrors, or otherwise creates new exposure.
data_handling.no_retention: Findings live only for the participant's review and are purged after the deliverable is produced; ETL stores nothing.
data_handling.participant_only: Results are shown only to the participant, never shared or sold.
data_handling.enforcement: Implement as an ephemeral, auto-purging store scoped to the session; retention must be enforced in code, not by policy alone.

notes: Dr. Rao's CBRN/national-security backpack is monitoring and classification only. Uplift refusal must be enforced at the model layer, not assumed from source choice.
Ali Malik's OSINT sweep is opt-in, runs async from the moment of consent, and is governed by an ephemeral no-retention data-handling policy (nothing republished, nothing kept by ETL).

currency_policy.principle: CBRN and export-control sources are a mix of live feeds and versioned lists. Most control lists update only at regime plenaries or via Federal Register rules, so they must be re-pulled on a cadence, not assumed static.
currency_policy.live: WHO Disease Outbreak News, CDC HAN, IAEA ITDB, BIS Federal Register rule changes, Consolidated Screening List, NRC ADAMS.
currency_policy.refresh_after: Australia Group / NSG / MTCR / Wassenaar plenary updates, OPCW Annex changes, Federal Select Agent list revisions, ODNI annual threat assessment release.
currency_policy.recommended_cadence: Weekly for live feeds; re-validate versioned control lists monthly and immediately after any regime plenary or Federal Register amendment.

intake_flow.host: Iris S. King hosts the Corridor. Her welcome panel sits at the top of the Corridor landing page in her own voice and register: she orients the visitor, explains what the Corridor is, and surfaces the OSINT option as a natural aside (not a banner or separate system prompt). Her card stays in the grid as a link to her full office, but her landing-page presence is the greeter role.
intake_flow.opt_in_gate: Inline beneath Iris's welcome: Ali Malik's subject-identifier form, collapsed by default. Expand -> provide the name -> submit -> confirmation that Ali is running. Skip -> proceed to Ms. Ivy with no friction.
intake_flow.iris_aside: One thing before you start: Dr. Malik at the end of the corridor runs a live search on whoever is presenting. It takes a few minutes. If you want it, give him the name now and he will have results waiting by the time you get there.
intake_flow.order: Iris S. King (host / greets at intake) -> Ms. Ivy (Concept Integrity) -> Dr. Sahini Rao (Dual-Use Systems, second stop) -> corridor specialist offices (Structure/Alicia James, Legal/Kimberly Pass, Human Factors/Sasha Moreno, Financial/Leo Vance, Risk Discipline/Rowan Tate, Discovery/Jax Rivera, Visual/Yuki Mendel) -> 16 sector judges -> Dr. Ali Malik (OSINT, final desk) -> consolidated findings.
intake_flow.ali_office: Ali Malik's office page has no gate; it opens straight to the flanking layout. If the visitor submitted through Iris, the brief panel is pre-populated with the subject dossier. If they skipped, the brief panel placeholder prompts them to enter the subject there.
intake_flow.note: Ali Malik runs asynchronously from the moment of opt-in at intake so his exposure map is ready when the visitor reaches the final desk.
